coredata.dc:title: Graphene oxide induces apoptotic cell death in endothelial cells by activating autophagy via calcium-dependent phosphorylation of c-Jun N-terminal kinases
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2016 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Lim, Mi-Hee
Jeung, In Cheul
Jeong, Jinyoung
Yoon, Sung-Jin
Lee, Sang-Hyun
Park, Jongjin
Kang, Yu-Seon
Lee, Hansu
Park, Young-Jun
Lee, Hee Gu
Lee, Seon-Jin
Han, Baek Soo
Song, Nam Woong
Lee, Sang Chul
Kim, Jang-Seong
Bae, Kwang-Hee
Min, Jeong-Ki
coredata.dc:description:
Despite the rapid expansion of the biomedical applications of graphene oxide (GO), safety issues related to GO, particularly with regard to its effects on vascular endothelial cells (ECs), have been poorly evaluated. To explore possible GO-mediated vasculature cytotoxicity and determine lateral GO size relevance, we constructed four types of GO: micrometer-sized GO (MGO; 1089.9±135.3nm), submicrometer-sized GO (SGO; 390.2±51.4nm), nanometer-sized GO (NGO; 65.5±16.3nm), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). All types but GQD showed a significant decrease in cellular viability in a dose-dependent manner. Notably, SGO or NGO, but not MGO, potently induced apoptosis while causing no detectable necrosis. Subsequently, SGO or NGO markedly induced autophagy through a process dependent on the c-Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK)-mediated phosphorylation of B-cell lymphoma 2 (Bcl-2), leading to the dissociation of Beclin-1 from the Beclin-1–Bcl-2 complex. Autophagy suppression attenuated the SGO- or NGO-induced apoptotic cell death of ECs, suggesting that SGO- or NGO-induced cytotoxicity is associated with autophagy. Moreover, SGO or NGO significantly induced increased intracellular calcium ion (Ca2+) levels. Intracellular Ca2+ chelation with BAPTA-AM significantly attenuated microtubule-associated protein 1A/1B-light chain 3-II accumulation and JNK phosphorylation, resulting in reduced autophagy. Furthermore, we found that SGO or NGO induced Ca2+ release from the endoplasmic reticulum through the PLC β3/IP3/IP3R signaling axis. These results elucidate the mechanism underlying the size-dependent cytotoxicity of GOs in the vasculature and may facilitate the development of a safer biomedical application of GOs.
Statement of Significance Graphene oxide (GO) have received considerable attention with respect to their utilization in biomedical applications. However, GO-related safety issues concerning human vasculature are very limited. In this manuscript, we report for the first time the differential size-related biological effects of GOs on endothelial cells (ECs). Notably, Subnanometer- and nanometersized GOs induce apoptotic death in ECs via autophagy activation. We propose a molecular mechanism for the GO-induced autophagic cell death through the PLCβ3/IP3/Ca2+/JNK signaling axis. Our findings could be provide a better understanding of the GO sizedependent cytotoxicity in vasculature and facilitate the future development of safer biomedical applications of GOs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Cytotoxicity
Autophagy
Calcium ion
Endothelial cell

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of GOs with various sizes
Quantification of GFP–LC3 puncta formation and mRFP–GFP LC3 color changes
Induction of apoptosis by GOs in ECs
Induction of autophagy in SGO- or NGO-treated ECs
Inhibition of autophagy attenuates SGO- or NGO-induced apoptotic cell death
SGO- and NGO -induced autophagy is dependent on JNK signaling pathway activation, but independent of reactive oxygen species or AMPK
JNK induces autophagy through phosphorylation of Bcl-2, leading to dissociation of Beclin-1 from the Beclin-1–Bcl-2 complex
SGO or NGO increases intracellular Ca2+ levels by activating calcium channels, and elevated intracellular Ca2+ activate subsequent downstream intracellular events related to GO-mediated autophagy

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2016 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Atomic force microscopy images and line profiles of four different forms of graphene oxide. (A) micrometer-sized graphene oxide (GO; MGO), (B) submicrometer-sized GO (SGO), (C) nanometer-sized (NGO), and (D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

Graphene oxide-mediated induction of apoptosis in endothelial cells. (A) Assessment of endothelial cell (EC) viability following treatment with GOs. Human umbilical vein ECs (HUVECs) were treated with the indicated concentrations of GOs for 24h and analyzed using the WST-1 assay. Quantitative data are reported as means±standard deviations (SD). *p<0.005 or **p<0.001 versus untreated controls. (B) Representative bar graph of the percentages of apoptotic cells (early apoptotic cell: annexin-V+/PI−, late apoptotic cell: annexin-V+/PI+) quantified by flow cytometric analysis. Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. *p<0.005 versus untreated controls. (C) Western blot analysis of caspase-3 activation in HUVECs following treatment with 25μg/mL GOs for 24h. (D and E) Effect of a pan-caspase inhibitor, z-VAD-fmk, on GO-induced apoptotic cell death. HUVECs were pretreated with 100-μM z-VAD-fmk or vehicle for 30min and subsequently treated with GOs at 25μg/mL for 24h. (D) Cell viability was determined using the WST-1 assay. Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. *p<0.005 versus vehicle control. (E) Relative caspase-3/7 activity. Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. *p<0.005 versus vehicle control.

Induction of autophagy in SGO- or NGO-treated ECs. (A) Western blot analysis of microtubule-associated protein 1A/1B-light chain 3 (LC3)-I to LC3-II conversion in HUVECs treated with GOs at the indicated concentrations for 24h. (B) Representative images of green fluorescent protein (GFP)–LC3 punctae in HUVECs after treatment with GOs at 25μg/mL for 12h (left panel). Scale bar=10μm. Bar graph indicates the number of GFP–LC3 dots per transfected cells (right panel). Quantitative data are reported as means±standard deviations. *p<0.005 versus untreated control. (C) Effect of a lysosomal inhibitor, Bafilomycin A1 (Baf A1), on SGO- or NGO-induced autophagy in HUVECs. HUVECs were pretreated with 10nM Baf A1 for 30min and subsequently treated with SGO or NGO for 5h. LC3-I to LC3-II conversion was detected by Western blot analysis. (D) Western blot analysis of the LC3-I to LC3-II conversion and p62 protein levels in HUVECs treated with 25μg/mL GOs for the indicated times. (E) Western blot analysis of cleaved GFP fragments from GFP–LC3. HUVECs transiently transfected with a GFP–LC3 plasmid were treated with 25μg/mL GOs for the indicated times and subjected to Western blotting with an antibody specific for GFP. (F) Representative images of mRFP–GFP–LC3 punctae. Colocalization of GFP and red fluorescent protein (RFP), indicated by yellow dots in the overlapped GFP and RFP images, is visible in autophagosomes (arrowhead), whereas only RFP fluorescence, indicated by red punctae, is observed in autolysosomes (arrow). Scale bar=10μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Inhibition of autophagy attenuates the SGO- or NGO-induced apoptotic cell death. (A) Western blot analysis of LC3-I to LC3-II conversion in HUVECs transfected with LC3-targeted or control siRNA. After 48h, control or LC3 siRNA-transfected HUVECs were treated with 25μg/mL SGO or NGO for 5h. Relative LC3-II to LC3-I ratios are indicated in the graph. Data were quantified using Image J software. (B) Western blot analysis of caspase-3 activation in control or LC3 siRNA-transfected HUVECs with 25μg/mL SGO or NGO for 24h. Cleaved caspase-3 band densities were analyzed using Image J software and normalized against β-actin expression. (C) Relative caspase-3/7 activity in control siRNA- or LC3 siRNA-transfected HUVECs. Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. #p<0.01 versus control siRNA. (D) Terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase-mediated deoxy uridine triphosphate nick end labeling (TUNEL) assay findings were analyzed using fluorescent microscopy (left panel, magnification; ×100). Blue fluorescence indicates DAPI nuclear staining; green fluorescence indicates TUNEL-positive nuclear staining. Bar graph shows the percentages of cells containing fragmented DNA (right panel). Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. #p<0.01 versus control siRNA. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

SGO- and NGO-induced autophagy depends on JNK signaling pathway activation. (A and C) Western blot analysis of p53 protein levels and p38, c-Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK), and extracellular-signal-regulated kinase (ERK) phosphorylation. HUVECs were treated with GOs at the indicated concentrations for 24h or for the indicated times with 25μg/mL GOs (M, MGO; S, SGO; and N, NGO). (B) Western blot analysis of p53 protein levels, AMPK phosphorylation, and LC3-I to LC3-II conversion. HUVECs were pretreated with 10-mM MG132 (proteasome inhibitor) for 30min and subsequently treated with SGO or NGO for the indicated times. (D and E) Western blot analysis of the LC3-I to LC3-II conversion following treatment with JNK inhibitor (SP600125) or JNK1/2 siRNA. Relative LC3-II to LC3-I ratios are indicated in the graphs. Data were quantified using the Image J software.

JNK induces autophagy in ECs through B-cell lymphoma 2 phosphorylation, leading to the dissociation of Beclin-1 from the Beclin-1–Bcl-2 complex. (A) Western blot analysis of B-cell lymphoma 2 (Bcl-2) phosphorylation. HUVECs were treated with SGO or NGO at 25μg/mL for the indicated times. (B) Coimmunoprecipitation of Beclin-1 and Bcl-2 from HUVECs treated with SGO or NGO for the indicated times. (C) Representative fluorescence image of Beclin-1 (Green) and Bcl-2 (Red) colocalization in HUVECs treated with 25μg/mL SGO or NGO for 30min (left panel). Bar graph of the percentage of Bcl-2 spots that colocalized with Beclin-1 (right panel). Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. #p<0.01 versus untreated control. Scale bar=10μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The role of Beclin-1 in SGO- or NGO-mediated induction of autophagy and apoptotic cell death. (A and B) Western blot analysis of the LC3-I to LC3-II conversion and caspase-3 activation in control siRNA- or Beclin-1 siRNA-transfected HUVECs. HUVECs were transfected with control siRNA or Beclin-1 siRNA; after 48h, cells were treated with 25μg/mL of SGO or NGO for 5h (A) or 24h (B). Relative LC3-II to LC3-I ratios and relative levels of cleaved caspase-3 are indicated in the graphs. Data were quantified using Image J software. Cleaved caspase-3 band densities were normalized against β-actin. (C) Relative caspase-3/7 activity in control siRNA- or Beclin-1 siRNA-transfected HUVECs. Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. #p<0.01 versus control siRNA. (D) TUNEL assay detection via fluorescent microscopy. Blue fluorescence indicates 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) nuclear staining; green fluorescence indicates TUNEL-positive nuclear staining (left panel). Percentage of cells that contain fragmented DNA (right panel, magnification; ×100). Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. #p<0.01 versus control siRNA. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

SGO and NGO induce autophagy through the PLCβ3/IP3/Ca2+/JNK signaling axis. (A) Histogram data from flow cytometric experiments reveal increased fluo4-NM fluorescence intensity in HUVECs treated with 25μg/mL GOs (left panel). The bar graph indicates the percentages of fluo4-NM positive cells (right panel). Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. #p<0.01 versus untreated control. (B and C) Western blot analysis of LC3-I to LC3-II conversion and JNK phosphorylation in SGO- or NGO-treated HUVECs in the presence or absence of BAPTA-AM. HUVECs were pretreated with BAPTA-AM for 30min, followed by an additional incubation with 25μg/mL SGO or NGO for 3h (B) or 30min (C). Relative LC3-II to LC3-I ratios are indicated in the graphs. Data were quantified using Image J software. The arrow indicates a nonspecific band. (D) HUVECs were pretreated with 2-APB for 30min, followed by an additional 30-min incubation with 25μg/mL SGO or NGO, followed by the quantitative estimation of changes in the mean fluorescence intensity of fluo4-NM. Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. #p<0.01 versus vehicle control. (E and F) HUVECs were transfected with control siRNA or PLCβ3 siRNA for 48h. (E) Reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) analysis of phospholipase C (PLC) β3 mRNA levels (upper panel). Quantitative estimates of changes in the mean fluorescence intensity of fluo4-NM in control siRNA- or PLC β3 siRNA-transfected HUVECs (lower panel). Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. #p<0.01 versus control siRNA (F) Western blot analysis of LC3-I to LC3-II conversion in control siRNA- or PLCβ3 siRNA-transfected HUVECs following treatment with SGO or NGO. (G) Determination of cell death in control siRNA- or PLCβ3 siRNA-transfected HUVECs following treatment with SGO or NGO. Quantitative data are reported as means±SD. #p<0.01 versus control siRNA.

Overview of GO-induced apoptotic cell death pathway in ECs. SGO and NGO bind to the cell membrane, leading to G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) stimulation. Following the activation of GPCR, the Gαq subunit induces PLC β3 activation, which hydrolyses phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate (PIP2) to diacylglycerol (DAG) and inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate (IP3). Subsequently, IP3 binds the IP3 receptor (IP3R) on the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) to induce Ca2+ efflux from ER. Subsequently, the increased intracellular Ca+2 level induced JNK phosphorylation, followed by Bcl-2 phosphorylation at serine 70 (S70) and dissociation of the Bcl-2–Beclin-1 complex. Following the dissociation of Beclin-1 from Bcl-2, activated Beclin-1 induces autophagy via LC3. As a result, autophagy induced by SGO or NGO induces apoptotic cell death via caspase-3 activation and DNA fragmentation.

head.ce:title: Graphene oxide induces apoptotic cell death in endothelial cells by activating autophagy via calcium-dependent phosphorylation of c-Jun N-terminal kinases

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biotherapeutics Translational Research Center, Korea Research Institute of Bioscience and Biotechnology, Daejeon, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Biotherapeutics Translational Research Center, Korea Research Institute of Bioscience and Biotechnology, 125 Gwahak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-806, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Research Institute of Bioscience and Biotechnology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite the rapid expansion of the biomedical applications of graphene oxide (GO), safety issues related to GO, particularly with regard to its effects on vascular endothelial cells (ECs), have been poorly evaluated. To explore possible GO-mediated vasculature cytotoxicity and determine lateral GO size relevance, we constructed four types of GO: micrometer-sized GO (MGO; 1089.9±135.3nm), submicrometer-sized GO (SGO; 390.2±51.4nm), nanometer-sized GO (NGO; 65.5±16.3nm), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). All types but GQD showed a significant decrease in cellular viability in a dose-dependent manner. Notably, SGO or NGO, but not MGO, potently induced apoptosis while causing no detectable necrosis. Subsequently, SGO or NGO markedly induced autophagy through a process dependent on the c-Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK)-mediated phosphorylation of B-cell lymphoma 2 (Bcl-2), leading to the dissociation of Beclin-1 from the Beclin-1–Bcl-2 complex. Autophagy suppression attenuated the SGO- or NGO-induced apoptotic cell death of ECs, suggesting that SGO- or NGO-induced cytotoxicity is associated with autophagy. Moreover, SGO or NGO significantly induced increased intracellular calcium ion (Ca2+) levels. Intracellular Ca2+ chelation with BAPTA-AM significantly attenuated microtubule-associated protein 1A/1B-light chain 3-II accumulation and JNK phosphorylation, resulting in reduced autophagy. Furthermore, we found that SGO or NGO induced Ca2+ release from the endoplasmic reticulum through the PLC β3/IP3/IP3R signaling axis. These results elucidate the mechanism underlying the size-dependent cytotoxicity of GOs in the vasculature and may facilitate the development of a safer biomedical application of GOs.

Graphene oxide (GO) have received considerable attention with respect to their utilization in biomedical applications. However, GO-related safety issues concerning human vasculature are very limited. In this manuscript, we report for the first time the differential size-related biological effects of GOs on endothelial cells (ECs). Notably, Subnanometer- and nanometersized GOs induce apoptotic death in ECs via autophagy activation. We propose a molecular mechanism for the GO-induced autophagic cell death through the PLCβ3/IP3/Ca2+/JNK signaling axis. Our findings could be provide a better understanding of the GO sizedependent cytotoxicity in vasculature and facilitate the future development of safer biomedical applications of GOs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene comprises a two-dimensional, single-atom-thick sheet made of planar sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, with a large surface area on both sides of the planar axis [1]. Graphene is part of a much broader graphene family of nanomaterials (GFNs) that include single-layer graphene, few-layer graphene (2–10 graphene layers), graphene oxide (GO; normally a single layer), reduced GO (normally a single layer), and graphite [2]. In recent years, graphene and its derivatives have received considerable attention with respect to their utilization in biomedical applications such as biosensing [3], drug delivery [4,5], diagnosis [6], bio-imaging, and tissue engineering [7,8]. Such applications are possible because the extremely high specific surface areas of graphene or GO can interact with various biomolecules and colloidal dispersions of individualized graphene sheets or GO can be easily engineered without metallic impurities [9]. In the field of biomedical applications, GO is becoming the favored form of GFN because of its better solubility/dispersibility in water and under physiological conditions than native graphene [2,10,11]. Therefore, the biomedical applications of GO are expected to rapidly expand in the near future. Despite this interest, however, GO-related safety issues concerning human health have not yet been fully evaluated, and more exhaustive nanotoxicological and human safety studies are urgently required.

Recently, several studies were conducted to assess the in vitro and in vivo toxic effects of GO, but contradictory results were obtained; some studies clearly showed no particular risks, whereas others indicated that GO contributes to health hazards [12–15]. The toxicities and biological responses of GO may depend on intrinsic factors such as lateral size, stiffness, hydrophobicity, surface functionalization, dose administered, and purity [2,16,17]. Among these, lateral size has been reported to differentially regulate cellular responses, and these experimental data provide invaluable clues for the appropriate design and engineering of GO with the intent to target cells or subcellular organelles. Yue et al. demonstrated that at different lateral sizes (350nm and 2μm), GO exhibited different initial cell interaction processes and intracellular compartmentalization in phagocytic cell lines. In addition, cellular responses such as inflammatory cytokine release are dependent on the sizes of GO nanosheets [15]. However, the effect of GO lateral size on cellular responses is poorly understood and must be urgently addressed.

Other studies have shown that GO may induce size- and dose-dependent cytotoxicities in various cell lines, although some results are contradictory with regard to the type of cell, conditions of cell culture, chemical nature of cell surface membranes, and expression of genes in each cell line [12,13]. Currently, the mechanisms of GO-induced cytotoxicity in various cell lines have not been fully elucidated. However, the depletion of mitochondrial membrane potential, which increases the generation of intracellular reactive oxygen species and triggers apoptosis through the activation of the mitochondrial pathway, is considered to be the most important mechanism underlying GO-induced cytotoxicity [18,19]. In addition, recent studies suggested that autophagy is another critical mechanism of GO-induced cytotoxicity; notably, Chen et al. reported the induction of autophagy and toll-like receptor (TLR) signaling, leading to inflammatory responses, in murine macrophage-like RAW264.7 cells after incubation with GO nanosheets [12].

Autophagy is considered to be a cytoprotective process involved in the normal turnover of long-lived proteins and whole organelles to maintain a healthy cellular status [20]. However, recent data strongly demonstrate that autophagy is intimately linked to apoptosis or necrosis and serves both pro-survival and pro-death functions. Autophagy regulation requires an orchestrated interplay between many signaling molecules, including mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) kinase, which has the most potent impact on autophagy [21,22]. Once activated, mTOR inhibits autophagy via the phosphorylation of autophagy-related proteins. AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) activation can lead to autophagy by negatively regulating mTOR [23,24]. The tumor suppressor protein p53 can trigger autophagy by phosphorylating AMPK and further inhibiting the mTOR signaling pathway [23]. Beclin-1 also plays a critical role in autophagosome formation and crosstalk between autophagy and apoptosis [24]. The BH3 domain-mediated binding of Beclin-1 to B-cell lymphoma 2 (Bcl-2) and B-cell lymphoma-extra large (Bcl-XL) inhibits autophagy. However, the c-Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK) 1- or extracellular-signal-regulated kinase (ERK)-mediated phosphorylation of Bcl-2 or death-associated protein kinase-mediated phosphorylation of Beclin-1 induces the dissociation of the Beclin-1–Bcl-2/Bcl-XL complex, thus inhibiting autophagy [24–28]. Intracellular calcium ions (Ca2+) can regulate the activation of JNK and the apoptotic signaling pathway [29].

Future biomedical applications of GO nanosheets will require frequent, systemic intravascular administration as GO nanosheet trafficking and systemic translocation are conducted by blood cells in circulation. GO nanosheets should also interact with or traverse the endothelial cell (EC) linings of blood vessels to function in target cells or tissues. Moreover, because blood vessels are critical for maintaining homeostasis in the human body, ECs should be protected from potential damage. Therefore, safety studies involving human primary blood components and ECs should be performed prior to expanding the human use of GO nanosheets. Several studies have reported that GO and its derivatives adversely affect erythrocytes and platelets as well as dendritic cells and monocytes/macrophages [13,14,30,31]. However, the current understanding of GO-mediated cytotoxicity in ECs is limited and warrants further detailed investigation.

This study aimed to evaluate the effects of different-sized GOs on EC cytotoxicity and to elucidate the mechanism(s) underlying GO-mediated cellular damage. We demonstrate for the first time that submicro- and nano-sized GOs induced apoptotic cell death by triggering autophagy in ECs. Calcium-dependent JNK activation, leading to the phosphorylation of Bcl-2 and subsequent dissociation of Beclin-1 from the Beclin-1–Bcl-2 complex, may establish the mechanism of GO-induced autophagy in ECs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of GOs with various sizes
ce:section.ce:para: GOs were prepared according to a modified version of the method published by Hummer [32]. Graphite (2g, 500 mesh; Sigma, St Louis, MO, USA) and sodium nitrate (1g, Sigma) were added to a concentrated H2SO4 solution (50mL) and stirred for 30min in an ice bath to keep the temperature below 5°C. Next, 0.3g increments of KMnO4 were added over 30min with continuous stirring and the temperature held below 10°C until a total of 7g of KMnO4 was added. After further stirring for 2h until the temperature reached 35°C, 90mL of water was slowly dripped into the solution, which had achieved a paste-like consistency. As the temperature of the solution further increased to 70°C, the solution was further stirred for 15min, followed by reaction termination by adding a mixture of 7mL of 30% H2O2 and 55mL of water. The suspension was washed three times by centrifugation (3000rpm, 10min) with a solution of 3% aqueous HCl, followed by drying in a vacuum evaporator to obtain GO powder. MGO was prepared by vortexing this GO powder in distilled water (1mg/mL) for 3min. SGO and NGO were prepared by sonicating the MGO solution in a bath sonicator (UC-02/220V, 60Hz/120W; Jeio Tech, Inc., Billerica, MA, USA) for 5min and 3h, respectively. After sonication, the SGO solution was centrifuged at 1000rpm for 1min to remove the remaining MGO, and NGO was filtered through a 0.45μm cellulose acetate membrane syringe filter. GQDs were prepared according to the method of Shen et al. [33]. In brief, 50mg of MGO was added to an aqueous nitric acid solution (2.5M, 40mL) and refluxed at 80°C for 24h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was sonicated for 2h, followed by adjustment to a pH of 8 by adding Na2CO3 (4.75g). The solution was filtered through a polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane filter (0.45μm) to remove larger GOs and was dialyzed for three days to remove the remaining chemicals. The concentrations of all GO solutions were adjusted to 1mg/mL in distilled water. The sizes and zeta potentials of the various GOs dispersed in distilled water were analyzed using a particle size analyzer (ELS-Z; Otsuka, Japan). Reported values were calculated as the averages of three individual measurements. TEM images were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai F20; Philips, Amsterdam, Netherlands) at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. GO thicknesses were measured using tapping-mode atomic force microscopy (MFP-3D; Asylum, Santa Barbara, CA, USA). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using an LS55 fluorescence spectroscope (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out using Thermo MultiLab 2000 spectrometer with Al cathode as the X-ray source (maximum 400W).

HUVECs were isolated from human umbilical cord veins by collagenase treatment, as previously described [34]. HUVECs at passages 2–7 were used for experiments. Cells were cultured in M199 medium supplemented with 20% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum, 1×Antibiotic-Antimycotic, 3ng/mL of basic fibroblast growth factor, and 5 units/mL of heparin. Before GO treatment, HUVECs were starved for 3h with M199 media containing 1% FBS.

HUVECs (1×104 cells/well in 200μL of HUVEC culture medium) were seeded in a 96-well plate and incubated for 24h at 37°C. After serum starvation, GO-containing media were added at different concentrations. After a 24h incubation, cytotoxicity was assessed by WST-1.

HUVECs transiently transfected with target-specific siRNA were seeded in a 35-mm dish and incubated overnight. Cells were subsequently treated with GOs for 24h, after which floating and adherent cells were collected and suspended in 50μL of PBS. The cells were then mixed with 50μL of 0.4% trypan blue solution, and dead cells were counted under an inverted microscope. Results are expressed as the ratio of the number of dead cells following GO treatment relative to the number of untreated control cells.

HUVECs were treated with 25μg/mL of GOs for 24h, after which caspase-3/7 activity was measured using Caspase-Glo 3/7 assay systems (Promega), according to the manufacturer’s instructions. In brief, equal volumes of Caspase-Glo 3/7 reagent were added to lysates, followed by incubation at room temperature for 3h. The luminescence of each sample was measured using a microplate luminometer. To determine DNA fragmentation resulting from the apoptotic signaling cascade, a TUNEL assay was performed using the DeadEnd Fluorometric TUNEL system kit. HUVECs were seeded in gelatin-coated eight-well chamber slides and incubated with GOs for 24h. Cells were fixed with 4% formaldehyde for 25min at 4°C and then permeabilized in 0.2% Triton X-100 for 5min at room temperature. Thereafter, cells were incubated with a TUNEL reaction mixture containing terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase and fluorescenin-12dUTP for 60min at 37°C in a humidified chamber.

For the Annexin V-FITC and PI assay, HUVECs were pre-incubated for 24h in 60-mm dishes. The medium was subsequently replaced with 2mL of medium containing GOs and incubated for 24h. At the end of the incubation, the cells were washed with PBS, harvested, and double-stained with Annexin V-FITC and PI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of GFP–LC3 puncta formation and mRFP–GFP LC3 color changes
ce:section.ce:para: One microgram of GFP-LC3 expression vector was transfected into HUVECs using the Neon transfection system (Life Technologies, USA). After a 12h, HUVECs were treated with GOs for 12h and subsequently fixed with 3.7% formaldehyde. Cell images were acquired via fluorescent microscopy and quantified using the Metamorph software. For quantification, GFP–LC3 punctae were determined in triplicate by counting the total number of punctae in more than 10 cells. To assess autolysosome formation, 1μg of mRFP-GFP-LC3 expression vector was transfected into HUVECs using the Neon transfection system. Transfected cells were incubated for 12h and subsequently treated with GOs for 12h. After treatment, cells were fixed with 3.7% formaldehyde, and images were obtained via florescence microscopy and analyzed using the Metamorph software.

For knockdown experiments, siRNA was transfected into HUVECs using the Neon transfection system. In brief, cells were washed with M199 media, resuspended in 100μL of R buffer (provided in the Neon kit), and mixed with 100pmol of siRNA before electroporation. After a 48h incubation, the cells were subjected to GO treatment for the indicated times.

Intracellular Ca2+ was detected via flow cytometry and fluorescence microscopy following staining with Fluo4-NM (Molecular Probes), a Ca2+-sensitive fluorescent indicator. For flow cytometry, cells were seeded in 60mm dishes and allowed to adhere overnight, followed by treatment with GOs for 30min and Fluo4-NM loading in the dark at 37°C for 30min. Cells were collected, and fluorescence intensity was measured using a flow cytometer. Additionally, fluorescent images were visualized under a fluorescence microscope.

GO-treated HUVECs were washed with PBS and subjected to RNA extraction using Trizol (Life Technologies), according to the manufacturer’s instructions. For cDNA synthesis, 2μg of total RNA was reverse-transcribed using oligo (dT)18 and Superscript III reverse transcriptase (Life Technologies), according to the manufacturer’s instructions. For the PCR amplification of target genes, 1μL of cDNA was used for each 20-μL HiPi PCR PreMix system-based reaction (ElpisBio, EBT-1202), according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

The following primers were used: PLC β1, 5′-GTG TCC GAC AGC CTC AAG AA-3′ (forward), 5′-ATC CCT GAG GGT CAG TCC TC-3′ (reverse); PLC β2, 5′-GAA GCT GGA GGA GAA GCAGG-3′ (forward), 5′-TCT GCC TCC AGA CCC TTC AT-3′ (reverse); PLC β3, 5′-CTT GCG GGT CAA GGT GAT CT-3′ (forward), 5′-GGC AGA CGT AGT GGT ATC CG-3′ (reverse); PLC β4, 5′-GCG GGT ACC TTC TCA AAC CA-3′ (forward), 5′-GTC AGT GGG CAA CCC ATA CA-3′ (reverse); XBP-1, 5′-TTA CGA GAG AAA ACT CAT GGC C-3′ (forward), 5′-GGG TCC AAG TTG TCC AGA ATG C-3′ (reverse); and GAPDH, 5′-ACA CGT TGG CAG TGG GGA CA-3′(forward), 5′-TGC CTC CTG CAC CAC CAA CT-3′ (reverse).

Quantitative data were expressed as means±standard deviations based on at least triplicate observations from three independent experiments. Differences between groups were analyzed using Student’s t-test. A p-value of <0.01 was considered to be statistically significant.

With the exception of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), GOs were prepared at different sizes using a modified version of the methods reported by Hummer, with additional sonication [32]. The size of GOs except GQD was determined by measuring the hydrodynamic diameter of GOs dispersed in aqueous solution. The average size of freshly prepared GOs was 1089.9±135.3nm; this was further decreased to 390.2±51.4nm and 65.5±16.3nm after sonication for 5min and 180min, respectively (Supplementary Fig. 1). These GOs were referred to as micrometer-sized GO (MGO), submicrometer-sized GO (SGO), and nanometer-sized GO (NGO), respectively. GQDs were prepared by refluxing GO with nitric acid to yield particles approximately 5nm in size (similar to the reported size of GQDs produced by Shen et al. [33]) as determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Supplementary Fig. 2A); these particles also exhibited distinctive blue fluorescence specific to GQDs (Supplementary Fig. 2B). The atomic force microscopic image analyses of four different sized GOs revealed distinctively that they are thin sheet-shaped and single-layered GOs with approximately 1.0nm thickness. GOs featured negative zeta-potential values of −22.35±0.71mV, −35.20±0.81mV, −31.08±0.49mV, and −34.35±0.64mV for GQD, NGO, SGO, and MGO, respectively (Fig. 1 ). These characteristics of GOs suggest hydrophilicity and good dispersion in an aqueous solution, which may be advantageous for the utilization of GOs in biomedical applications such as bioimaging, drug delivery, and phototherapy. Furthermore, the chemical composition of GOs with various size was analyzed by XPS. The C1s XPS-spectra of GOs except GQD showed the significant peaks at 284.1eV, 285.5eV, and 287.0eV corresponding to C–C=C, C-O, and C=O, respectively (Supplementary Fig. 3), indicating that NGO, SGO, and MGO were partially oxidized. In the spectrum of GQD, the peaks at 286.4eV and 289.3eV corresponding to C-O and C=O, respectively, were significant, indicating that GQD was highly oxidized due to secondary oxidation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Induction of apoptosis by GOs in ECs
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the biological activities of GOs with different sizes on primary human umbilical vein ECs (HUVECs), cells were treated with different concentrations (5, 10, or 25μg/mL) of MGO, SGO, NGO, or GQD for 24h, after which cellular viability was determined. MGO, SGO, and NGO, but not GQD, induced significant decreases in cellular viability in a dose-dependent manner (Fig.2 A and Supplementary Fig. 4A). To clarify whether these GO-mediated decreases in cellular viability resulted from the induction of apoptosis or necrosis, HUVECs were treated with GOs as indicated, stained with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC)-conjugated annexin V and propidium iodide (PI), and subjected to flow cytometric analysis. SGO or NGO markedly induced apoptosis in ECs, whereas MGO induced necrosis rather than apoptosis in a dose-dependent manner (Fig.2B and Supplementary Fig. 4B). In SGO- or NGO-treated ECs, caspase-3/7 activation was substantially increased as assessed by the pro-caspase-3 cleavage and caspase-3/7 activity assays (Fig.2C and Supplementary Fig. 4C). Moreover, pretreatment with z-VAD-fmk, a pan-caspase inhibitor, markedly blocked the SGO- or NGO-mediated decreases in cellular viability and increased caspase-3/7 activity (Fig.2D and E), but had no effect on cellular viability in MGO-treated ECs (Fig.2D), indicating that SGO or NGO induces apoptotic cell death in ECs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Induction of autophagy in SGO- or NGO-treated ECs
ce:section.ce:para: Increasing evidence has shown that autophagy and apoptosis can act cooperatively, antagonistically, or synergistically to regulate cell fates, depending on the specific cellular context [23,35,36]. To explore whether SGO- or NGO-induced apoptotic cell death in ECs resulted from an interplay between autophagy and apoptosis, we evaluated the effects of GOs on the induction of autophagy in HUVECs. The conversion of microtubule-associated protein 1A/1B-light chain 3 (LC3)-I to LC3-II, a hallmark of autophagy, was markedly increased in ECs treated with SGO or NGO in a dose-dependent manner, but not in cells treated with MGO or GQDs (Fig.3 A). Autophagy induction was further supported by the following findings: when green fluorescent protein (GFP)–LC3 fusion proteins were expressed in ECs, markedly increased numbers of cytoplasmic punctae, due to GFP–LC3 accumulation in autophagosomes, were observed in SGO- or NGO-treated ECs; in contrast, a ubiquitous, diffuse pattern of cytosolic green fluorescence was observed in control and MGO- or GQD-treated ECs (Fig.3B).

Although increased numbers of autophagosomes, indicated by the accumulation of LC3-II or increased numbers of GFP-LC3 punctae, suggest the induction of autophagy, this phenomenon is not always indicative of autophagy induction and may represent either enhanced autophagosome generation or a block in both autophagolysomal maturation and the autophagy pathway completion. Therefore, additional experimental methods are needed to distinguish between the induction of autophagy and inhibition of downstream steps such as the impairment of lysosomal function and/or fusion of autophagosomes with lysosomes [37]. In this context, we performed turnover assays for LC3 and other autophagy substrates, such as p62, to determine whether the overall autophagic flux had been induced. As shown in Fig.3C, ECs treated with SGO or NGO in the presence of bafilomycin A1 (Baf A1), a lysosomal inhibitor, exhibited marked increases in LC3-II accumulation, indicating that increased amounts of LC3 in autophagosomes had already been delivered to lysosomes for degradation. In addition, the total cellular expression levels of p62 decreased in a time-dependent manner (Fig.3D) in SGO- or NGO-treated ECs, indicating an increase in autophagic activity because p62 is selectively incorporated into the autophagosome through direct binding to LC3 and is efficiently degraded by autophagy [38].

Autophagy induction in SGO- or NGO-treated ECs was further confirmed through a GFP-LC3 cleavage assay as free GFP fragments that resulted from GFP–LC3 degradation in autolysosomes could be detected in those cells via immunoblotting with anti-GFP antibodies (Fig.3E). Differences in the stability of GFP and red fluorescent protein (RFP) in acidic compartments may lead to the quenching of GFP fluorescence but the clear detection of RFP fluorescence in autolysosomes. Accordingly, a tandem RFP–GFP–LC3 construct could differentially label autophagosomes and autolysosomes with yellow and red signals, respectively. In ECs treated with SGO or NGO, substantial increases in both yellow and red punctae were observed relative to the control, indicating that autophagic flux had increased in those cells (Fig.3F). Collectively, these results provide strong evidence suggesting that SGO or NGO, but not MGO or GQD, induce autophagy in ECs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of autophagy attenuates SGO- or NGO-induced apoptotic cell death
ce:section.ce:para: Next, to examine the role of autophagy in SGO- and NGO-induced apoptotic cell death, HUVECs were treated with SGO or NGO after transfection with LC3-specific small interfering RNA (siRNA). LC3-specific siRNA treatment blocked SGO- or NGO-induced autophagy as determined by the reduced LC3-II accumulation in those cells (Fig.4 A). This impaired SGO- or NGO-induced autophagy following transfection with LC3-specific siRNA resulted in a significant inhibition of pro-caspase-3 cleavage and caspase-3/7 enzymatic activity (Fig.4B and C), and consequent apoptotic cell death as assessed using terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase-mediated deoxy uridine triphosphate nick end labeling (TUNEL) and trypan blue exclusion assays and in comparison with ECs transfected with control siRNA (Fig.4D and Supplementary Fig. 5). Taken together, these results clearly suggest that SGO or NGO promote apoptotic cell death through autophagy induction in ECs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SGO- and NGO -induced autophagy is dependent on JNK signaling pathway activation, but independent of reactive oxygen species or AMPK
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we scrutinized the signaling pathways that play critical roles in SGO- or NGO-mediated autophagy induction in ECs. The core molecular machinery involved in autophagosome formation and the signaling pathways that stimulate autophagy have been studied in detail. Among these, reactive oxygen species (ROS) are considered to be among the most potent regulators of autophagy (Supplementary Fig. 6A and B). ROS can be generated either as a byproduct of oxygen metabolism in the electron transport chain within mitochondria or through the activity of cellular enzymes such as NADPH oxidases [39]. To determine whether SGO- or NGO-induced ROS generation was involved in autophagy induction, HUVECs were pretreated with a mitochondria-targeted ROS scavenger, Mito-tempo, or a potential NADPH oxidase inhibitor, apocynin. Although both SGO and NGO induced intracellular ROS generation, this did not appear to affect the induction of autophagy because treatment with Mito-tempo or apocynin did not impair the accumulation of LC3-II in SCO- or NGO-treated HUVECs (Supplementary Fig. 6C).

Recently, cytoplasmic p53 was reported to regulate apoptosis and block autophagy through the inhibition of the AMPK/mTOR-signaling pathway, although the relationship between autophagy and p53 remains complicated [23]. We next examined the levels of p53 in GO-treated HUVECs to evaluate the potential involvement of p53 in GO-mediated autophagy induction. A marked decrease in p53 protein expression was observed in SGO- or NGO-treated ECs, whereas no significant differences were observed in MGO- or GQD-treated ECs (Fig.5 A), suggesting that a decrease in p53 triggers AMPK activation, thus inhibiting mTOR signaling and inducing autophagy. Unexpectedly, no significant change in AMPK phosphorylation was observed in SGO- or NGO-treated HUVECs, despite the decreased p53 levels in those cells (Fig.5B). In addition, treatment with the proteasome inhibitor MG132, in an attempt to recover p53 protein levels, did not affect the GO-mediated induction of autophagy (Fig.5B). Collectively, these results indicate that SGO or NGO induce autophagy in a ROS- or AMPK-independent manner.

Next, we determined the effects of GOs on the activation of mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signaling in ECs. As shown in Fig.5C, treatment with SGO or NGO markedly induced the phosphorylation of p38 MAPK and JNKs but not ERKs, whereas MGO had no marked effects on MAPK signaling pathways. We tested the effects of SGO and NGO on autophagy induction in the presence of specific MAPK signaling pathway inhibitors. SGO- or NGO-mediated LC3-II accumulation was significantly attenuated by treatment with the JNK inhibitor SP600125, but not the p38 inhibitor SB203580 (Fig.5D). Similarly, the transfection of ECs with JNK-specific siRNA after treatment with SGO or NGO significantly inhibited the accumulation of LC3-II (Fig.5E), indicating that SGO- or NGO-induced autophagy is mediated by JNK signaling pathway activation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: JNK induces autophagy through phosphorylation of Bcl-2, leading to dissociation of Beclin-1 from the Beclin-1–Bcl-2 complex
ce:section.ce:para: The functional and structural interactions between Beclin-1 and Bcl-2 family proteins, including Bcl-2, are well established. Notably, Bcl-XL is a partial mediator of the crosstalk between autophagy and apoptosis, and Beclin-1 can bind to and is inhibited by Bcl-2 or Bcl-XL. On the phosphorylation of Bcl-2 family proteins, however, Beclin-1 dissociates from the complex formed with these proteins and subsequently induces autophagy [24]. Taken together with the finding that JNK signaling plays a critical role in SGO- or NGO-mediated autophagy activation, we considered that JNK may contribute to GO-induced autophagy through the phosphorylation of Bcl-2, thus leading to the dissociation of Beclin-1. The levels of the phosphorylated (activated) form of Bcl-2 (at Ser70) were highly increased following treatment with SGO or NGO, whereas the total Bcl-2 protein level was not affected by GO treatment (Fig.6 A). GO treatment also affected the interaction between Beclin-1 and Bcl-2 in ECs as assessed using an immunoprecipitation assay (Fig.6B). Similarly, significant decreases in Bcl-2 and Beclin-1 co-localization signals were observed in SGO- and NGO-treated ECs relative to controls. An immunofluorescence double co-localization experiment (Fig.6C) revealed that these decreases were due to the dissociation of Beclin-1 from the complex, thus suggesting that JNK regulates SGO- or NGO-mediated autophagic activity in ECs through Bcl-2 phosphorylation, leading to the dissociation of Beclin-1 from the Beclin-1–Bcl-2 complex.

To further clarify the role of Beclin-1 in SGO- or NGO-mediated autophagy induction and apoptotic cell death, HUVECs were treated with Beclin-1-specific siRNA, and the effects of SGO and NGO treatment on autophagy and/or apoptosis were examined in these cells. Compared to control ECs, Beclin-1 knockdown reduced LC3-II accumulation after treatment with SGO or NGO (Fig.7 A). The inhibition of autophagy in ECs in response to Beclin-1 depletion also led to a decrease in SGO- or NGO-mediated apoptosis as determined via pro-caspase-3 cleavage, caspase-3/7 activity assays, TUNEL staining, and trypan blue exclusion assays (Fig.7B–D and Supplementary Fig. 7). All these results strongly suggest that JNK plays an essential regulatory role in the SGO- or NGO-mediated induction of autophagy and apoptosis through the phosphorylation of Bcl-2, resulting in the dissociation of Beclin-1 from the Beclin-1–Bcl-2 complex and the subsequent induction of Beclin-1-mediated autophagy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SGO or NGO increases intracellular Ca2+ levels by activating calcium channels, and elevated intracellular Ca2+ activate subsequent downstream intracellular events related to GO-mediated autophagy
ce:section.ce:para: Ca2+ has long been known to play an important role in numerous cellular processes, including cell death regulation, and therefore, the cytosolic Ca2+ concentration should be tightly regulated. Notably, the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) and mitochondria serve as primary Ca2+ storage organelles [29,40]. As Ca2+ is a key signaling molecule upstream of MAPKs, we hypothesized that SGO or NGO induces JNK phosphorylation and consequent autophagy activation in ECs by increasing intracellular Ca2+ levels. An apparent increase in Ca2+ levels was observed in SGO- or NGO-treated ECs, whereas no significant changes were found in MGO- or GQD-treated ECs (Fig.8 A and Supplementary Fig. 8A).

To further determine the role of intracellular Ca2+ in SGO- or NGO-mediated autophagy activation, HUVECs were pre-incubated with BAPTA-AM, a highly selective cell-permeant chelator of intracellular Ca2+, for 30min before treatment with SGO or NGO; LC3-II accumulation and JNK phosphorylation were subsequently measured. As shown in Fig.8B and C, the chelation of intracellular Ca2+ with BAPTA-AM apparently suppressed JNK phosphorylation and LC3-II accumulation. The inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor (IP3R), a Ca2+ channel, is activated by IP3 binding and regulates Ca2+ levels in the cytosol. The inhibition of IP3R with 2-aminoethoxydiphenyl borate (2-APB) apparently attenuated the SGO- and NGO-induced elevation of intracellular Ca2+ levels, whereas extracellular Ca2+ removal using ethylene glycol tetraacetic acid (EGTA) had no significant effects (Supplementary Fig. 8B), indicating that the increased intracellular Ca2+ levels observed following SGO or NGO treatment is released from internal ER pools to the cytosol (Fig.8D).

Phospholipase C (PLC) catalyzes the hydrolysis of phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate to yield diacylglycerol and IP3 [41]. Once generated, IP3 quickly diffuses in the cytoplasm and activates IP3R, evoking a rapid release of Ca2+ into the cytoplasm. Notably, PLC family members possess distinct mechanisms of activation. For example, PLC γ isoforms are activated by receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs), PLC β isoforms are activated by the Gα and Gβ/γ subunits of heterotrimeric G proteins, and PLC ε can be activated by both types of molecules [42]. As we did not observe significant RTK activation in GO-treated ECs (Supplementary Fig. 9A) and as PLC ε and PLC ζ are not expressed in HUVECs [43], we hypothesized that PLC β isoforms are activated in response to GO treatment, resulting in Ca2+ release from ER. Accordingly, we examined the expression of PLC β isoforms in ECs. Reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) analysis revealed the prominent expression of gene transcripts encoding PLC β1 and β3 in ECs (Supplementary Fig. 9B). Notably, the knockdown of PLC β3 significantly reduced SGO- and NGO-induced Ca2+ release, LC3-II accumulation, and consequent apoptotic cell death (Fig.8E–H), whereas no significant effects were observed in PLC β1 siRNA-transfected ECs (Supplementary Fig. 10). Taken together, these results strongly indicate that the SGO- or NGO-increased intracellular calcium mobilization are regulated through PLC β3, leading to autophagy induction and apoptotic cell death in ECs.

Human exposure to GFNs, such as GO nanosheets, has rapidly increased in response to their expanding applications in the biomedical field. The evaluations of safety issues and their impacts on human health, such as recent reports on adverse effects of GFNs, have always presented challenges [44]. In this regard, an understanding of the causative relationship between the intrinsic biological properties of GFNs (i.e., sizes, shapes, and surface physicochemical properties) and their effects on human health is urgently required to ensure safer biomedical applications of these nanomaterials in the future [2]. Although surface area is considered to be an important parameter with respect to GFN cytotoxicity, toxicological properties associated with size among GFNs with the same chemical composition and the underlying molecular mechanisms of action remain largely unexplored. Moreover, the biomedical application of GFNs may require systemic intravascular administration. Therefore, ECs that line blood vessels would be the primary site of contact with GFNs. However, the current understanding of the effects of GFNs on ECs and therefore on blood vasculature is very limited.

Among GFNs, GO contains hydrophilic oxygen atoms in the forms of carboxyl, epoxy, and hydroxyl groups on the basal plane and edges, rendering GO amenable to stable dispersion in water and functionalization. These attributes have prompted the use of GO in biomedical applications [18]. To explore possible GO-mediated cytotoxicity in blood vasculature, particularly with regard to GO size-dependent cellular responses, four types of GOs with different lateral were prepared. Of these four types, SGO and/or NGO induced significant cytotoxicity in ECs via apoptotic cell death.

Increasingly, nanotoxicological studies have shown that many GFNs can modulate autophagy in many different types of cells; as a result, autophagy is currently under consideration as a critical mechanism of GFN-induced cellular cytotoxicity [12,45]. Although autophagy plays a central role in regulating cellular homeostasis, prolonged or uncontrolled autophagy can also promote cell death during normal development as well as in disease [46]. In cells treated with GFNs, autophagy would likely be induced as a potential defense mechanism to eliminate foreign materials and protect cells from GFN-induced cellular damage. However, it is also possible that GFNs induce cell death by promoting autophagy. Therefore, the net outcome of GFN-induced autophagy induction (e.g., pro-survival and/or pro-apoptotic) should be considered as the mutual relationship between autophagy and apoptosis is highly dependent on context. In the present study, we observed that SGO or NGO, but not MGO or GQDs, induced apoptotic death in ECs via autophagy activation, whereas autophagy blockade using LC3-specific siRNAs significantly reduced apoptotic cell death after treatment with SGO or NGO. Although an earlier report described autophagic cell death as caspase independent [47], we observed a significant decrease in caspase-3/7 activity levels in ECs transfected with LC3-specific siRNAs prior to treatment with SGO or NGO (Fig.4B and C). Collectively, these results indicate that autophagy triggers GO-mediated apoptotic cell death by exerting a pro-apoptotic function in ECs.

GOs of different sizes have been reported to induce apoptotic cell death and/or autophagy in various types of cells, including macrophages, human embryonic kidney cells, and cancer cells [12,15,48]. However, most of these studies failed to elucidate the detailed molecular mechanisms underlying GO-induced autophagy and the effects on biological dysfunction in ECs. Accordingly, to our knowledge, the present report is the first to demonstrate the autophagy-mediated induction of apoptotic death in ECs in response to GOs of different sizes. Moreover, this study provides further insights into the mechanism by which SGO and NGO induce autophagy in ECs.

ROS, MAPK signaling, and p53-mediated AMPK/mTOR signaling have been previously described to be critical to the regulation of cell death and autophagy [23,39,49]. However, in the present study, the scavenging of ROS, blockade of proteasome-mediated p53 degradation, and pharmacological inhibition of NADPH oxidase or p38 MAPK had no significant effects on SGO- or NGO-mediated autophagy induction (Fig. 5 and Supplementary Fig. 6), whereas the pharmacological inhibition or siRNA knockdown of JNKs significantly decreased LC3-II accumulation following SGO or NGO treatment. These results indicate that SGO or NGO activate autophagy-associated signal pathways in a context-dependent manner and that JNK plays a critical role in autophagy activation in ECs. JNK activation in response to SGO or NGO treatment appears to induce autophagy in ECs through Bcl-2 phosphorylation and consequent dissociation of the Beclin-1–Bcl-2–Bcl-XL complex, consistent with data presented by Wei et al. [27]. In addition to maintaining the anti-apoptotic function of Bcl-2, the binding of Bcl-2 to Beclin-1 can block the Beclin-1-mediated induction of autophagy; in the present study, Beclin-1 depletion in ECs significantly decreased SGO- or NGO-mediated autophagy induction and apoptotic death (Fig. 7 and Supplementary Fig. 7). Present data demonstrating SGO- or NGO-mediated autophagy activation via JNK-induced Bcl-2 phosphorylation, leading to the dissociation of the Beclin-1–Bcl-2 complex, may provide promising insights for future biomedical applications because this signaling pathway may be targeted as a key factor in the mitigation of GO-induced autophagic death in ECs.

According to a recent report, GO induces autophagy in macrophages by modulating the TLR4 and TLR9 signaling pathways [14]. In addition, the tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α)/tumor necrosis factor receptor (TNFR) signaling axis can also provoke autophagy induction in various cells, including human and murine macrophages, human T lymphoblastic leukemic cells, human vascular smooth cells, human skeletal muscle cells, and rat epithelial cells [36,50,51]. In this context, we determined whether TLRs or TNF-α/TNFR signaling pathways were involved in SGO- or NGO-mediated autophagy induction in ECs. The blockade of TLR4 signaling with a TRL4 inhibitor, CLI-095, had no significant effects on SGO- or NGO-induced LC3-II accumulation and caspase-3/7 activity in ECs (Supplementary Fig. 11A and B). Moreover, treatment with a TNFR inhibitor, R7050, also failed to inhibit SGO- or NGO-induced LC3-II accumulation (Supplementary Fig. 11C). These results strongly suggest that SGO- or NGO-induced autophagy of ECs is modulated by signaling pathways involving molecules other than TLRs or TNF-α/TNFR.

ER facilitates the proper folding of synthesized proteins and serves as an intracellular Ca2+ store. The ability of cells to respond to perturbations in ER function, or ER stress, is critical for cell survival; however, chronic or unresolved ER stress can trigger autophagy. In response to ER stress, the activation of a specific nuclear signaling pathway, called the unfolded protein response, results in reduced global protein synthesis and increased corrective protein (such as chaperones) production. In mammalian cells, ER stress is sensed by three upstream signaling proteins, PERK, ATF6, and IRE1, which sense the levels of unfolded proteins in the ER lumen and pass this information to the cytoplasm and nucleus. Protein kinase R-like endoplasmic reticulum kinase (PERK) phosphorylates the α-subunit of the translation initiation factor eukaryotic initiation factor 2 (eIF2), which attenuates global translation initiation during autophagy induction. eIF2 phosphorylation by PERK leads to the increased expression of activating transcription factor (ATF) 4 and its downstream target CHOP, which participates in ER-stress-corrective actions. ATF6 plays an important role in chaperone induction and CHOP transcriptional upregulation. Inositol-requiring enzyme 1 (IRE1) activation, a stress-activated endoribonuclease resident in ER, may lead to the splicing of a critical transcription factor, X-box binding protein 1 (XBP-1). The spliced form of XBP-1 induces the transcription of genes involved in almost all aspects of the unfolded protein response. Moreover, phosphorylated and activated IRE1 can activate JNK; given the link between sustained JNK activity and cell death [29], JNK activity might thereby connect IRE1-mediated ER stress signaling to cell death [52,53]. However, these three stress transducer proteins did not appear to affect SGO- or NGO-mediated autophagy induction as no significant changes in the levels of spliced XBP1 mRNA, IRE1 phosphorylation, or CHOP expression were observed in SGO- or NGO-treated ECs (Supplementary Fig. 12). Rather, SGO- or NGO-mediated autophagy induction appears to be regulated by Ca2+ released from ER through PLC-mediated signaling events.

Ca2+ is a major regulator of autophagy, and several types of nanoparticles modulate Ca2+ homeostasis [54] and induce autophagy in association with apoptosis [35,54–56]. Moreover, intracellular Ca2+ can also modulate the phosphorylation/dephosphorylation of protein kinases and promote signal transduction pathways that ultimately activate JNK as well as caspase-3 [29]. Accordingly, perturbed calcium homeostasis may affect apoptosis induction in ECs [40]. Calcium homeostasis is regulated by several calcium channels, and one of the best-known mechanisms of autophagy induction via calcium channel activity modulation involves the IP3–Beclin 1–Bcl-2 pathway, an mTOR-dependent pathway driven by ER stress. Upon binding to IP3, IP3R can affect autophagy in at least two ways: by direct binding to Beclin-1 via the receptor N-terminal region or by AMPK-dependent inhibition of the mTORC1 pathway [57]. However, increases in the cytoplasmic Ca2+ level, mediated via IP3R, play a role in either the inhibition or activation of autophagy, according to the cellular state [58]. In the present study, we clearly demonstrated that treatment with SGO or NGO, but not MGO or GQDs, significantly increased intracellular Ca2+ levels, whereas intracellular Ca2+ chelation with BAPTA-AM significantly attenuated LC3-II accumulation and JNK phosphorylation, thus decreasing autophagy induction. Moreover, PLC β3 inhibition significantly suppressed SGO- or NGO-induced Ca2+ release from ER to the cytosol, suggesting that SGO and NGO induce autophagy in ECs through the PLCβ3/IP3/Ca2+/JNK signaling axis.

Size-dependent differential cellular responses to GOs were also reported by Yue et al. [15], who demonstrated that although GOs ranging in size from 2μm to 350nm were equally well internalized in phagocytic cells such as macrophages, intracellular event and cytokine profiles were significantly regulated by the lateral dimensions of GOs. These authors reported negligible GO internalization in non-phagocytic cells, including cancer cells and ECs, which might be attributable to the strong electrostatic repulsions between GOs and negatively charged cell surfaces. Although we demonstrated that GOs induce apoptotic cell death via autophagy activation in ECs and that the PLCβ3/IP3/Ca2+/JNK signaling axis may play an important role in this process, the detailed molecular mechanisms by which GOs interact with cells and exert their functions in non-phagocytic ECs is unclear and remains to be elucidated. Moreover, the performances of GOs or their functional derivatives with respect to other parameters, such as surface properties, should be also explored in the future.

Particular biological responses against GFNs may elicit toxic responses in some situations, but might represent advantages with respect to therapeutic modalities in other situations. For example, SGO- or NGO-mediated induction of autophagic cell death is harmful in terms of applications in healthy human bodies, but may be beneficial in terms of anti-angiogenic approaches to cancer treatment. In this context, the data presented herein may help establish new guidelines for the selection of appropriately sized GOs in future biomedical applications, although this field remains in its infancy.

In this study, we presented experimental data regarding the differential size-related biological effects of GOs on ECs. Additionally, we demonstrated for the first time that GOs induce apoptotic death in ECs via autophagy activation. As presented in Fig. 9 , SGOs or NGOs induces autophagic cell death through the PLCβ3/IP3/Ca2+/JNK signaling axis. Although further investigations are required before the current findings can be clinically applied, these data will provide a better understanding of the mechanism underlying GO size-dependent cytotoxicity in vasculature and facilitate the future development of safer biomedical applications of GOs.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by grants from the Korea Research Institute of Bioscience and Biotechnology; by the National Research Foundation of Korea, which was funded by the Ministry of Science, Information & Communication Technology and Future Planning (grants NRF-2011-0020507, NRF-2012M3A9C6050331, NRF-2012M3A9C7050101, NRF-2013M3A9B6046566, NRF-2014M3A7B6034497, and NRF-2015M3A9C6030284); and by the Creative Allied Project and National Agenda Project through the National Research Council of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.actbio.2016.09.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biological interactions of graphene-family nanomaterials: an interdisciplinary review
Nitrogen-doped graphene and its application in electrochemical biosensing
Chitosan-functionalized graphene oxide as a nanocarrier for drug and gene delivery
Magnetic Fe3O4-graphene composites as targeted drug nanocarriers for pH-activated release
New horizons for diagnostics and therapeutic applications of graphene and graphene oxide
Two-dimensional nanostructure-reinforced biodegradable polymeric nanocomposites for bone tissue engineering
Understanding the pH-dependent behavior of graphene oxide aqueous solutions: a comparative experimental and molecular dynamics simulation study
Safety considerations for graphene: lessons learnt from carbon nanotubes
Biomedical applications of graphene and graphene oxide
Simultaneous induction of autophagy and toll-like receptor signaling pathways by graphene oxide
Cytotoxicity of graphene oxide and graphene in human erythrocytes and skin fibroblasts
Graphene oxide induces toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4)-dependent necrosis in macrophages
The role of the lateral dimension of graphene oxide in the regulation of cellular responses
Protein corona-mediated mitigation of cytotoxicity of graphene oxide
Cytotoxicity effects of graphene and single-wall carbon nanotubes in neural phaeochromocytoma-derived PC12 cells
A systems toxicology approach to the surface functionality control of graphene-cell interactions
Minimizing oxidation and stable nanoscale dispersion improves the biocompatibility of graphene in the lung
The role of autophagy in vascular biology
Regulation mechanisms and signaling pathways of autophagy
Inhibition of mTOR induces autophagy and reduces toxicity of polyglutamine expansions in fly and mouse models of Huntington disease
Docosahexaenoic acid induces autophagy through p53/AMPK/mTOR signaling and promotes apoptosis in human cancer cells harboring wild-type p53
Self-consumption: the interplay of autophagy and apoptosis
Activation of ERK-p53 and ERK-mediated phosphorylation of Bcl-2 are involved in autophagic cell death induced by the c-Met inhibitor SU11274 in human lung cancer A549 cells
